## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a qualified professional. Our data is current as of 2026 and regularly reviewed.

faq.a: One joule (1 J = 1 kg·m²/s²) is the energy required to apply one newton of force over one meter of distance.

faq.q: How does kinetic energy change with velocity?
faq.a: Kinetic energy increases with the square of velocity. Double the speed and energy increases four-fold—which is why higher speeds are exponentially more dangerous.

faq.q: How much kinetic energy does a car have?
faq.a: A 1,000 kg car at 50 km/h has 96 kJ. At 100 km/h, it has 386 kJ—four times more. This shows why stopping distance increases dramatically at higher speeds.

faq.q: What happens to kinetic energy in a collision?
faq.a: Kinetic energy converts into other forms: metal deformation, sound, and heat. Just 100 kJ causes significant structural damage.

faq.q: Is kinetic energy different for rotating objects?
faq.a: Yes, rotational kinetic energy uses a different formula: Ec_rot = ½·I·ω², where I is moment of inertia and ω is angular velocity.
